behaviors.description: TSP heuristic produces routes ≤1.5x optimal for inputs up to 20 stops
behaviors.justification: This is a pure algorithm test. The failure being caught is: the heuristic produces sub-optimal routes. To catch this, we need known test inputs (coordinate sets), run the real heuristic function, measure route length, and compare to optimal/benchmark. This is a single function in isolation with no I/O, external APIs, or LLM dependence. Mocking the heuristic would hide the failure entirely.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a fixed set of test coordinates (up to 20 stops) with known optimal route lengths (from benchmarks or brute-force for small cases). Call the TSP heuristic function. Measure the returned route's total distance. Assert that distance ≤ 1.5 × optimal for all test cases.

behaviors.description: Time window estimates computed correctly from distance and speed: time = distance / configured_speed
behaviors.justification: The behavior tests a formula: time = distance / speed, given two inputs. The failure being caught is: the formula is implemented wrongly (e.g., speed / distance, wrong units, missing road-type adjustment). We can test this with mocked distance values and known speed configurations. No external I/O or LLM is needed. A Unit test provides a known distance (either mocked or from test coordinates) and speed, calls the estimation function, and verifies the time matches the formula.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the distance calculation to return known values (e.g., 10 km). Provide a test speed config (e.g., 50 km/h). Call the time-window estimation function. Assert time = distance / speed (e.g., 10/50 = 0.2 hours). Repeat with multiple road types and speeds to verify the road-type lookup works.

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects routes exceeding driver's remaining shift time and returns excess amount
behaviors.justification: This is pure validation logic: if total_time > remaining_hours, reject and return excess. The failure being caught is: the validator has the condition wrong (e.g., < instead of >), or returns the wrong excess amount. Inputs (total_time, remaining_hours) are controlled by the test. No external dependencies, I/O, or LLM. A simple Unit test with known numeric inputs will catch logic errors.
behaviors.test_description: Create test routes with known total times (e.g., 8 hours, 10 hours). Provide driver shift hours (e.g., 8 hours). Call the validator. Assert rejection when total > remaining. Assert acceptance when total ≤ remaining. Assert excess = total - remaining when rejected (e.g., excess = 10 - 8 = 2).

behaviors.description: When geocoding fails for an address, service skips that stop and optimizes remaining, reporting skipped addresses
behaviors.justification: This tests error handling across multiple service components. The service must call a geocoding API, detect failure (no results), skip the failed address, re-optimize the remaining, and report which was skipped. A pure Unit test (mocking the geocoding function in isolation) would miss integration failures: e.g., wrong parameters sent to the API, missing validation of the API response, incorrect filtering of failed addresses. A mock that only patches the function could hide issues in how the service orchestrates across geocoding + filtering + optimization. Integration test with a mocked/stubbed geocoding API (that simulates no-results response) is needed to verify the full workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of addresses (valid + one that geocoding will fail on). Mock the geocoding API to return empty/no-results for the bad address. Call the service. Assert the response includes: (1) optimized route with only valid addresses, (2) a report listing the skipped address by name/identifier. Verify the remaining addresses are still properly optimized.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses consolidated into single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice
behaviors.justification: This is pure deduplication logic. The failure being caught is: the service doesn't detect duplicates, or creates multiple stops for the same address. Inputs (list of addresses with duplicates) are controlled; no external APIs, I/O, or LLM. A Unit test directly calls the deduplication logic with known duplicate addresses and verifies the result is a single consolidated stop with combined items.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of delivery items with duplicate addresses (e.g., '123 Main St' appears twice with different item sets). Call the service's consolidation logic. Assert the output has exactly one stop for '123 Main St'. Assert the items are merged (e.g., [item1, item2] from the two instances are combined into one entry).
